title: Syncope in an anticoagulated elder with a racing pulse and black stools

chart.oneLiner: 74M with atrial fibrillation on apixaban, CAD, CKD, and type 2 diabetes presents after collapsing at home; he has felt increasingly tired and lightheaded for days, takes daily ibuprofen and aspirin for knee pain, and on arrival has an irregular, rapid pulse.

opening: I got up off the couch, everything went grey, and the next thing I knew my wife was shaking me on the floor.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 74-year-old man, pale, tired-looking, and a little short of breath. A reasonable historian once prompted, but tends to blame everything on his bad knee and his heart 'doing its thing again.'

HPI: Four to five days of progressive fatigue, exertional breathlessness, and lightheadedness, capped today by a syncopal episode as he stood up from the couch. There was a brief grey-out with no warning palpitations he remembers, no chest pain, no tongue-biting, no incontinence, and he came round within a minute or so feeling washed-out (no prolonged confusion). On directed asking: he has noticed BLACK, TARRY, foul stools for about three days (melena) and some dull burning in the upper belly that he put down to 'indigestion.' NO vomiting of blood or coffee-grounds that he noticed, NO bright red blood per rectum, NO fevers. He felt his heart 'fluttering' on and off for a couple of days but says it does that with his atrial fibrillation. The faint was preceded by lightheadedness on standing (postural).
MEDS (reveal on direct questioning about medicines/blood thinners/pain pills): apixaban (a blood thinner) for atrial fibrillation; daily low-dose aspirin; ibuprofen most days for knee arthritis, sometimes two or three times a day; metoprolol for rate control; lisinopril for blood pressure; metformin for diabetes; and sertraline started about two months ago for low mood. He is not always sure he remembers his evening apixaban. NO warfarin.
PMH: atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VASc high, on apixaban), coronary artery disease with a prior stent, chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine ~1.3), type 2 diabetes, hypertension, osteoarthritis. SOCIAL: retired machinist; 2-3 beers most evenings; former smoker. FAMILY: noncontributory. ROS: fatigue, exertional dyspnea, lightheadedness, melena, mild epigastric burning as above; otherwise negative.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale, fatigued, mildly diaphoretic, slightly breathless at rest.
- Vitals: BP 98/61, HR 128 and irregularly irregular, RR 20, 36.7C, SpO2 97% RA; marked orthostatic drop with reproduction of symptoms if checked.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, irregularly irregular, no new murmur; JVP low/flat. Abdomen: soft, mild epigastric tenderness, no rebound or guarding, no pulsatile mass.
- RECTAL EXAM: black, tarry, melenic stool on the glove if performed - a key finding.
- Conjunctival and palmar pallor if examined; cool peripheries with sluggish capillary refill if assessed.
- No focal neurologic deficit; no asterixis.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the black stools, the anticoagulant/aspirin/NSAID use, or the epigastric burning unless asked about stool color/GI symptoms, medications/blood thinners/pain relievers, or abdominal symptoms.
- Do NOT volunteer that he sometimes misses his apixaban dose unless asked specifically about how reliably he takes his medicines.
- The melena is revealed only if the student performs (or asks for) a rectal exam.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.Glucose: Fingerstick glucose 142 - mildly elevated (known diabetic, stress); not contributing to syncope.
labs.CBC: Hgb 7.4 (low; reported baseline ~13), MCV 78 (low - microcytic, suggesting chronic iron loss superimposed on an acute bleed), WBC 9.2, Plt 264. Significant anemia.
labs.BMP: Na 138, K 5.4 (high), Cl 104, HCO3 19 (low) -> anion gap = 138 - (104 + 19) = 15 (mildly elevated). BUN 52, Cr 1.8 (baseline ~1.3) -> BUN:Cr = 52 / 1.8 = 29 (markedly elevated, >20), consistent with an upper GI bleed plus prerenal AKI on CKD. The hyperkalemia reflects AKI plus lisinopril; not yet EKG-manifest.
labs.Lactate: Lactate 3.1 mmol/L (elevated) - tissue hypoperfusion from hypovolemia; explains the mildly elevated anion gap.
labs.Coags: INR 1.6 and a measurable anti-Xa (apixaban) effect, accentuated by renal impairment - a pharmacologic bleeding diathesis rather than intrinsic coagulopathy. Not on warfarin.
labs.Troponin: High-sensitivity troponin mildly elevated and on a slow rise (type 2 / demand pattern) - supply-demand mismatch from anemia and rate-related ischemia, not a primary plaque-rupture MI.
labs.TypeCross: Type and crossmatch sent; 2 units pRBC requested and held.
labs.Orthostatics: Marked orthostatic drop with reproduction of the patient's symptoms.
labs.StoolEvaluation: Melena (black, tarry stool) documented on rectal exam - indicates a (likely upper) GI bleeding source. Provided as a clinical/diagnostic finding.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response (~125-135 bpm): irregularly irregular narrow-complex rhythm, absent organized P waves, fibrillatory baseline, with rate-related horizontal ST-segment depression in the lateral leads (I, aVL, V5-V6) - a demand-ischemia pattern, not an acute STEMI. No peaked T waves despite K 5.4.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related lateral ST depression. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) - (c) Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and lateral ST depression
searchTerms: atrial fibrillation rapid ventricular response ECG
AF with rate-related ST depression demand ischemia
ekg.clinicalVignette: An older anticoagulated man with known atrial fibrillation presents pale and lightheaded with an irregularly irregular, fast pulse.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Clear lung fields, no pulmonary edema or consolidation; normal cardiomediastinal silhouette; no free air under the diaphragm; incidental old healed rib fractures with callus along the lateral ribs.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: clear lungs, no free air under the diaphragm, incidental healed lateral rib fractures. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph with clear lungs, no free air, and old healed rib fractures
searchTerms: normal PA chest radiograph no free air
cxr.clinicalVignette: An older man with a fall and syncope has a screening chest film to exclude an acute cardiopulmonary process and free air.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this elderly man's syncope and note your leading diagnosis. He has known atrial fibrillation and arrived with a fast, irregular pulse, so explicitly name three distinct mechanisms by which his syncope could be cardiac/arrhythmic versus hypovolemic, and say which you favor and why.
steps.rubric: Hypovolemic syncope from acute GI blood loss is the leading & CANT-MISS diagnosis (8). The genuine competing fork - primary cardiogenic/arrhythmic syncope, here atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response causing inadequate cardiac output - must be raised as CANT-MISS (5). Names three distinct cardiac-vs-hypovolemic mechanisms (e.g. tachyarrhythmia/AF-RVR with poor filling; bradyarrhythmia/conduction block or pauses; structural - aortic stenosis or ischemia; versus hypovolemia/orthostasis from blood loss) (core, up to 3). Orthostatic hypotension (core,2). Anemia of another (chronic) cause (core,2). Vasovagal/situational, less likely (core,1). Connects pallor + tachycardia + orthostasis + (on history) melena/NSAID/anticoagulant use to occult GI bleeding and argues the rapid AF is a RATE RESPONSE to anemia/hypovolemia rather than the primary cause (core,4). PENALTY: anchoring on 'just his AF acting up' and missing the bleed (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Hypovolemic syncope from acute (likely upper) GI blood loss - pallor, orthostasis, tachycardia, and on history melena with NSAID/aspirin/anticoagulant use. The genuine competing diagnosis is primary cardiogenic/arrhythmic syncope given his known atrial fibrillation and rapid irregular pulse; this must be excluded. Three distinct cardiac-vs-hypovolemic mechanisms to name: (1) tachyarrhythmia - atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response shortening diastolic filling and dropping output; (2) bradyarrhythmia/conduction disease - high-grade AV block or sinus pauses (he is on metoprolol); (3) structural/ischemic - aortic stenosis or demand ischemia reducing forward flow - versus the hypovolemic/orthostatic mechanism of acute blood loss. Favor hypovolemic GI bleed, and argue the AF with rapid ventricular response is most likely a secondary RATE response to anemia and volume depletion rather than the primary driver. Also reasonable: orthostatic hypotension, anemia of another cause, vasovagal (less likely).

criticalActions.item: Hypovolemic syncope from acute GI blood loss (leading)
Primary cardiogenic/arrhythmic syncope - AF with rapid ventricular response (competing fork)

coreActions.item: Names three distinct cardiac-vs-hypovolemic mechanisms (tachyarrhythmia; brady/conduction; structural/ischemic vs hypovolemia)
Connects pallor + tachycardia + orthostasis + melena/NSAID/anticoagulant use to occult GI bleeding and frames the rapid AF as a secondary rate response

penalties.item: Anchors on 'his AF acting up' and misses the bleed

mustNotMiss: Primary cardiogenic/arrhythmic syncope - AF with rapid ventricular response

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order? Don't forget the bedside.
steps.rubric: CBC for anemia (CANT-MISS,4). Recognizes GI bleeding and performs/reviews a rectal exam for melena (CANT-MISS,4). 12-lead EKG given the irregular tachycardia and AF history (2). BMP - note BUN:Cr ratio, AKI, and potassium (2). Type and crossmatch (1). Coagulation studies / assess the apixaban effect (1). Lactate and troponin (1). Orthostatic vitals (1). CXR (1). PENALTY: working up syncope without a rectal exam / not checking a hemoglobin (-3); reflexively cardioverting or heavily rate-controlling the AF before assessing volume/bleeding (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; fingerstick Glucose; CBC; BMP (BUN:Cr, AKI, potassium); Lactate; Troponin; Coagulation studies including assessment of the apixaban/anti-Xa effect; Type & crossmatch with units held; orthostatic vitals; and a rectal exam to document stool color. Resist treating the AF rate as the primary problem until volume status and bleeding are assessed.

criticalActions.item: Rectal exam to document melena / recognizes GI bleeding

penalties.item: Working up syncope without a rectal exam / not checking a hemoglobin
Cardioverting or heavily rate-controlling the AF before assessing volume/bleeding

requiredTests: Rectal exam to document melena / recognizes GI bleeding
contraindicatedTests: Working up syncope without a rectal exam / not checking a hemoglobin
Cardioverting or heavily rate-controlling the AF before assessing volume/bleeding
orderSensitiveActions: Working up syncope without a rectal exam / not checking a hemoglobin

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's own 12-lead EKG, taken on arrival. Describe your findings and say how they change (or do not change) your leading diagnosis.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response (~125-135 bpm): irregularly irregular narrow-complex rhythm with no organized P waves and a fibrillatory baseline, plus rate-related horizontal ST depression in the lateral leads - a demand-ischemia pattern, NOT an acute STEMI; and notably NO peaked T waves despite a potassium of 5.4. CREDIT: identifies AF with rapid ventricular response (6); recognizes the lateral ST depression as RATE-RELATED demand ischemia rather than a primary occlusive MI, and interprets the fast AF as most likely a secondary response to anemia/hypovolemia (5); notes the absence of peaked T waves despite hyperkalemia / systematic read with rate, rhythm, axis, intervals (2); states this does not overturn the GI-bleed as the leading driver (2). PENALTY: calling the lateral ST depression a STEMI and rushing to cath, or treating the AF as a primary electrical emergency warranting immediate cardioversion in a bleeding patient (-3).
keyFindings: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
rate-related lateral ST depression = demand ischemia, not STEMI
no peaked T waves despite K 5.4
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related lateral ST depression (demand ischemia) - a secondary response to anemia/hypovolemia, not a primary STEMI.
commonMistakes: calling the lateral ST depression an acute STEMI and activating cath
treating the rapid AF as a primary electrical emergency in a bleeding patient
expecting (or inventing) peaked T waves because the potassium is 5.4
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response (~125-135 bpm): irregularly irregular narrow-complex rhythm, no organized P waves, fibrillatory baseline, with horizontal ST depression in the lateral leads (I, aVL, V5-V6). The ST depression is rate-related demand ischemia, not an acute occlusive STEMI, and there are NO peaked T waves despite the potassium of 5.4. This is best read as a SECONDARY tachycardic response to anemia and hypovolemia from blood loss, so it supports rather than overturns the GI-bleed as the leading cause; the fix is to treat the bleed and transfuse, not to cardiovert or aggressively rate-control a hypovolemic patient.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related lateral ST depression (demand ischemia) - a secondary response to anemia/hypovolemia, not a primary STEMI.
coreActions: atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
rate-related lateral ST depression = demand ischemia, not STEMI
interprets the fast AF as secondary to anemia/hypovolemia, supporting the GI-bleed

penalties.label: calls it a STEMI / treats the AF as a primary electrical emergency in a bleeding patient

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear lungs with no pulmonary edema or consolidation, normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, NO free air under the diaphragm, and incidental old healed rib fractures with callus along the lateral ribs. CREDIT: recognizes a non-acute film and specifically states there is no pulmonary edema and no subdiaphragmatic free air (5); identifies the healed rib fractures with callus as an old, incidental finding (4). PENALTY: calling the film acutely abnormal, mistaking the callus for a pulmonary/pleural lesion, or inventing edema/consolidation (-2).
keyFindings: clear lungs, no edema, no free air under the diaphragm
incidental old healed rib fractures with callus
criticalFindings: Clear film with no pulmonary edema and no subdiaphragmatic free air; incidental old healed rib fractures.
commonMistakes: mistaking callus for a pulmonary or pleural lesion
inventing pulmonary edema or an infiltrate on a clear film
steps.idealAnswer: Clear lung fields with no pulmonary edema or consolidation, a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette, and no free air under the diaphragm; incidentally there are old healed rib fractures with bony callus along the lateral ribs - an old, non-acute finding.

criticalActions: Clear film with no pulmonary edema and no subdiaphragmatic free air; incidental old healed rib fractures.
coreActions: clear lungs, no edema, no free air under the diaphragm
incidental old healed rib fractures with callus

penalties.label: calls the study acutely abnormal / invents edema or a lesion

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The labs are back: Hgb 7.4, BUN:Cr 52/1.8 (ratio ~29), K 5.4, lactate 3.1, a measurable apixaban effect, a mild type-2 troponin rise, and melena on rectal exam; the EKG shows AF with a rapid ventricular response and rate-related ST depression. Commit to your single most likely diagnosis, name the discriminating test that would confirm it, and explain how you resolved the bleed-versus-arrhythmia fork. Also state the other multi-system problems you must address concurrently.
steps.rubric: Commits to syncope from an acute (likely upper) GI bleed causing hypovolemia and anemia (4). Resolves the fork: the rapid AF and lateral ST depression are a SECONDARY rate response/demand ischemia from the bleed, not the primary cause; melena + Hgb 7.4 + BUN:Cr ~29 + the NSAID/aspirin/anticoagulant combination point to the GI source (3). Names UPPER ENDOSCOPY (EGD) as the discriminating/confirmatory test (2). Acknowledges and plans to address the concurrent multi-system problems - prerenal AKI on CKD with hyperkalemia (K 5.4), lactic acidosis from hypoperfusion, the supratherapeutic anticoagulant effect, and the type-2 troponin rise (1).
steps.idealAnswer: Single most likely diagnosis: syncope from an acute (likely upper) GI bleed causing hypovolemia and anemia. Reasoning and fork resolution: melena on rectal exam, an Hgb of 7.4, and a BUN:Cr of ~29 localize an upper GI source, and the daily ibuprofen + aspirin + apixaban (with missed-dose uncertainty) are the precipitant. The atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and the rate-related lateral ST depression are a SECONDARY tachycardic/demand response to anemia and volume loss, not the primary cause of the syncope - so I resolve the bleed-versus-arrhythmia fork toward the bleed. Discriminating/confirmatory test: upper endoscopy (EGD) to localize and treat the lesion. Concurrent multi-system problems to address: prerenal AKI on CKD with hyperkalemia (K 5.4 from the AKI plus lisinopril), a lactic acidosis (lactate 3.1, anion gap 15) from hypoperfusion, a pharmacologic bleeding diathesis from apixaban accentuated by renal impairment, and a type-2 troponin rise from demand ischemia.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan? Address how you handle the rapid AF in the setting of active bleeding, then describe your disposition and what specifically would have to be true for this patient to be SAFE for eventual discharge.
steps.rubric: Per MGH GI bleed: resuscitate - 2 large-bore IVs + crystalloid + type & crossmatch (CANT-MISS,5); transfuse pRBC to a restrictive threshold, Hgb>7, or >8 given CAD/active ischemia (CANT-MISS,4); HOLD aspirin/NSAIDs and the apixaban and REVERSE the anticoagulant effect (andexanet alfa or PCC) given active bleeding (CANT-MISS,4); manage the AF by treating the underlying cause (volume + transfusion) rather than aggressive nodal blockade or cardioversion in a hypovolemic, bleeding patient (3); IV PPI infusion and NPO (2); urgent GI consult for endoscopy (EGD) to localize/treat (2); treat the hyperkalemia (K 5.4) and hold the ACE inhibitor for the AKI (1); serial Hgb and telemetry monitoring (1); SAFE-DISCHARGE/disposition - admit (ICU/monitored bed if unstable), and articulate that safe discharge later requires hemodynamic stability with a stable Hgb off transfusion, completed endoscopic therapy, a deliberate anticoagulation restart plan with GI/cardiology, NSAID cessation with gastroprotection, medication reconciliation, and close follow-up (3). PENALTY: attributing the syncope to vasovagal or 'just AF' and discharging without recognizing the bleed (-4); giving a beta-blocker/calcium-channel blocker push or cardioverting the hypovolemic patient, or continuing/anticoagulating through the bleed (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Resuscitate: 2 large-bore IVs, isotonic crystalloid, type & crossmatch, transfuse pRBC to a restrictive threshold (Hgb >7, or >8 given his CAD and demand ischemia). STOP aspirin and all NSAIDs and HOLD the apixaban; reverse the anticoagulant effect with andexanet alfa (or 4-factor PCC if unavailable) because he is actively bleeding. Manage the rapid AF by correcting its cause - volume and transfusion - rather than pushing a beta-blocker/calcium-channel blocker or cardioverting a hypovolemic, bleeding patient; reserve gentle rate control for persistent rapid AF only once euvolemic. NPO; start an IV PPI infusion. Urgent GI consult for upper endoscopy (EGD) to localize and treat the lesion (colonoscopy if no upper source). Treat the hyperkalemia (K 5.4) and hold the lisinopril for the AKI; recheck electrolytes and renal function. Serial Hgb (it lags 24-72h) and continuous telemetry; trend the troponin as a type-2 rise that should resolve with treatment of the anemia. Disposition: admit to a monitored bed, ICU if unstable. Safe eventual discharge requires hemodynamic stability with a stable Hgb off transfusion, completed/secured endoscopic therapy, a deliberate shared anticoagulation-restart plan with GI and cardiology (balancing his high stroke risk against rebleeding), permanent NSAID cessation with gastroprotection, full medication reconciliation, and close GI/PCP/cardiology follow-up.

criticalActions.item: Resuscitate (2 large-bore IVs, crystalloid, type & crossmatch)
Transfuse to restrictive threshold (Hgb>7, >8 given CAD/ischemia)
Hold aspirin/NSAIDs and apixaban and reverse the anticoagulant (andexanet/PCC)

coreActions.item: Manage the AF by treating the cause (volume/transfusion), not cardioversion or aggressive nodal blockade in a hypovolemic bleeding patient
Treat hyperkalemia and hold the ACE inhibitor for AKI
Safe-discharge/disposition plan (admit now; later requires stable Hgb, completed endoscopy, anticoagulation-restart plan, NSAID cessation with gastroprotection, med reconciliation, close follow-up)

penalties.item: Attributing syncope to vasovagal/'just AF' and discharging / missing the bleed
Cardioverting or pushing nodal blockers in the hypovolemic patient, or anticoagulating through the bleed

requiredActions: Resuscitate (2 large-bore IVs, crystalloid, type & crossmatch)
Transfuse to restrictive threshold (Hgb>7, >8 given CAD/ischemia)
Hold aspirin/NSAIDs and apixaban and reverse the anticoagulant (andexanet/PCC)

Safe-discharge/disposition plan (admit now; later requires stable Hgb, completed endoscopy, anticoagulation-restart plan, NSAID cessation with gastroprotection, med reconciliation, close follow-up)
unsafeActions: Attributing syncope to vasovagal/'just AF' and discharging / missing the bleed
Cardioverting or pushing nodal blockers in the hypovolemic patient, or anticoagulating through the bleed

Cardiac: tachycardic, irregularly irregular, no new murmur; consistent with atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response.
Conjunctival and palmar pallor if examined.
Vitals: BP 98/61, HR 128 and irregularly irregular, RR 20, 36.7C, SpO2 97% RA; marked orthostatic drop with reproduction of the patient's symptoms if checked.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 74M with atrial fibrillation on apixaban, CAD, CKD, and type 2 diabetes presents after syncope, on a background of NSAID and aspirin use and days of melena. He arrives pale and orthostatic with atrial fibrillation and a rapid ventricular response. The diagnosis is Syncope from occult GI bleed: the rapid AF and rate-related ST depression are a secondary response to anemia and hypovolemia rather than the primary cause. It is reached by eliciting the focused history (stool color, blood thinners, NSAIDs, medication adherence), performing a rectal exam, reading the patient's own EKG as demand-driven AF, and integrating multi-system labs (anemia, prerenal AKI on CKD with hyperkalemia, lactic acidosis, an apixaban effect, and a type-2 troponin rise) while excluding primary cardiogenic/arrhythmic syncope.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: soft, mild epigastric tenderness, no rebound or guarding, no pulsatile mass.
Cardiac: tachycardic, irregularly irregular, no new murmur; consistent with atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response.
Conjunctival and palmar pallor if examined.
Vitals: BP 98/61, HR 128 and irregularly irregular, RR 20, 36.7C, SpO2 97% RA; marked orthostatic drop with reproduction of the patient's symptoms if checked.
Cool peripheries with sluggish capillary refill - peripheral hypoperfusion from hypovolemia.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals - an orthostatic, tachycardic, anticoagulated elder after syncope.
Elicit the focused history, including stool color, blood thinner/NSAID use, and medication adherence only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam, including orthostatic vitals and a rectal exam, to reveal pallor, hypoperfusion, and melena.
Build a differential led by hypovolemic syncope from GI bleeding while explicitly forking against primary cardiogenic/arrhythmic syncope (AF with RVR).

Read the patient's own EKG as AF with rapid ventricular response and rate-related demand ischemia, and integrate the multi-system labs (anemia, AKI with hyperkalemia, lactic acidosis, apixaban effect, type-2 troponin).
Commit to Syncope from occult GI bleed and manage with resuscitation, transfusion, anticoagulant reversal, PPI, endoscopy, cause-directed AF management, and a safe-discharge plan.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on 'his AF acting up' and missing the bleed
Calling the lateral ST depression a STEMI
Cardioverting or rate-controlling the hypovolemic patient instead of treating the bleed
Continuing or restarting anticoagulation through an active bleed
managementPearls: Resuscitate (2 large-bore IVs, crystalloid, type & crossmatch)
Transfuse to restrictive threshold (Hgb>7, >8 given CAD/ischemia)
Hold aspirin/NSAIDs and apixaban and reverse the anticoagulant (andexanet/PCC)
criticalWorkupItems: Rectal exam to document melena / recognizes GI bleeding
criticalInterpretationItems: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and rate-related lateral ST depression (demand ischemia) - a secondary response to anemia/hypovolemia, not a primary STEMI.
Clear film with no pulmonary edema and no subdiaphragmatic free air; incidental old healed rib fractures.

imageInterpretationPearls: A fast, irregularly irregular rhythm with rate-related ST depression in a pale, orthostatic patient is demand-driven AF - treat the cause (the bleed), not the tracing.
Hyperkalemia does not always show on the EKG; a K of 5.4 with no peaked T waves still needs treating.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Asks permission before sensitive questions (alcohol, medication adherence, rectal exam)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
